action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: lake, stone, boat, dog, hill, road, farmer, king
Adjectives: (this language has no adjectives)
Verbs (write them in this base form): feed, hold, carry, hear, see
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a lake feed a stone
    three lakes feed a stone

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    two boats see two dogs  =>  vulovu lu ruvukevu lu kupu
    a hill carry two stones  =>  lasovi porirevu lu kali
    three lakes carry a hill  =>  misilavu vave lasovi kali
    three hills hear two hills  =>  lasovivu vave lasovivu lu lavula
    three dogs feed three boats  =>  ruvukevu vave vulovu vave same
    two hills hold three hills  =>  lasovivu lu lasovivu vave vevo
    a king hold two stones  =>  rusi porirevu lu vevo
    three kings see a farmer  =>  rusivu vave vulu kupu
    three roads carry two stones  =>  peluruvu vave porirevu lu kali

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 4 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 3 English sentences (so at most
   12 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 3 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to establish the basic word order (SVO, SOV, etc.) and how subjects, objects, and verbs are marked. I will start by comparing sentences with the same subject and verb but different objects to see if the object changes position or form. I'll also check if the verb changes based on the subject or object.

Round 1 Strategy:
1. Compare "a lake feed a stone" (hypothetical) with known data. Actually, I don't have "a lake feed..." yet. I have "three lakes carry a hill" and "a hill carry two stones". Let's look at the verb "carry".
   - "three lakes carry a hill" => misilavu vave lasovi kali
   - "a hill carry two stones" => lasovi porirevu lu kali
   In both, "lasovi" is the noun for hill/stone? No, "lasovi" is hill in the second, but in the first "lasovi" is the object? Wait.
   Sentence 2: "a hill carry two stones" => lasovi porirevu lu kali. Here "lasovi" is likely the subject (hill). "porirevu" is carry. "lu kali" is two stones.
   Sentence 3: "three lakes carry a hill" => misilavu vave lasovi kali. Here "misilavu" is three lakes. "vave" is carry. "lasovi kali" is a hill.
   So "lasovi" is definitely "hill". "kali" seems to be "stone" (from "lu kali" = two stones).
   Wait, in sentence 2 "lu kali" = two stones. In sentence 3 "lasovi kali" = a hill. So "kali" is "stone" and "lasovi" is "hill".
   Let's re-examine sentence 2: "a hill carry two stones" => lasovi porirevu lu kali.
   Subject: a hill (lasovi). Verb: carry (porirevu). Object: two stones (lu kali).
   Sentence 3: "three lakes carry a hill" => misilavu vave lasovi kali.
   Subject: three lakes (misilavu). Verb: carry (vave). Object: a hill (lasovi kali).
   Note: "porirevu" vs "vave". The verb "carry" changes form! "porirevu" when subject is singular? "vave" when subject is plural? Or maybe object agreement?
   Let's check "see".
   "two boats see two dogs" => vulovu lu ruvukevu lu kupu.
   "three kings see a farmer" => rusivu vave vulu kupu.
   "three dogs feed three boats" => ruvukevu vave vulovu vave same.
   "three hills hear two hills" => lasovivu vave lasovivu lu lavula.
   
   Hypothesis on Word Order:
   In "lasovi porirevu lu kali" (S V O), order is S V O.
   In "misilavu vave lasovi kali" (S V O), order is S V O.
   In "vulovu lu ruvukevu lu kupu" (S V O), order is S V O.
   It seems S V O is the base order.

   Hypothesis on Verb Agreement:
   "carry":
   - S: a hill (sing) -> porirevu
   - S: three lakes (pl) -> vave
   - S: three dogs (pl) -> vave (in "three dogs feed three boats", verb is "vave")
   - S: three kings (pl) -> vave (in "three kings see a farmer", verb is "vave")
   - S: three hills (pl) -> vave (in "three hills hear two hills", verb is "vave")
   It looks like the verb changes based on the subject number. Singular subject -> specific form, Plural subject -> "vave"?
   Wait, "feed" in "two boats see two dogs" is "ruvukevu". "see" is "ruvukevu"? No, "see" is "ruvukevu" in "two boats see two dogs".
   Let's map verbs:
   feed: same (from "three dogs feed three boats" => ... same)
   hold: porirevu (from "a hill carry two stones" -> wait, "carry" is porirevu? Or is "hold" porirevu?)
   Let's re-read carefully.
   "a hill carry two stones" => lasovi porirevu lu kali. Verb = carry.
   "a king hold two stones" => rusi porirevu lu vevo. Verb = hold.
   So "porirevu" is used for both "carry" (with hill) and "hold" (with king). This suggests "porirevu" might be the verb for "hold" and "carry" is different?
   Wait, "three lakes carry a hill" => misilavu vave lasovi kali. Verb = carry = vave.
   "a hill carry two stones" => lasovi porirevu lu kali. Verb = carry = porirevu.
   Why the difference?
   Maybe it's object agreement?
   Obj: a hill (lasovi) -> vave? No, in "misilavu vave lasovi kali", obj is "lasovi kali".
   In "lasovi porirevu lu kali", obj is "lu kali" (two stones).
   Maybe the verb form depends on the object?
   Or maybe "porirevu" is "hold" and I misidentified "carry" in the second sentence?
   Let's check the list again.
   "a hill carry two stones" => lasovi porirevu lu kali.
   "a king hold two stones" => rusi porirevu lu vevo.
   Both use "porirevu". One is "carry", one is "hold". This implies "porirevu" could be a generic verb or I am misinterpreting the English input.
   Wait, the prompt says: "Verbs (write them in this base form): feed, hold, carry, hear, see".
   Maybe "carry" and "hold" map to the same form in this language? Unlikely for a logic puzzle.
   Alternative: The English sentence "a hill carry two stones" might actually be "a hill hold two stones"? No, I must trust the input.
   Let's look at "three dogs feed three boats" => ruvukevu vave vulovu vave same.
   Subject: three dogs (ruvukevu). Verb: feed (vave). Object: three boats (vulovu vave).
   Here "vave" is used for "feed" with plural subject.
   "two boats see two dogs" => vulovu lu ruvukevu lu kupu.
   Subject: two boats (vulovu lu). Verb: see (ruvukevu). Object: two dogs (lu kupu).
   Here "ruvukevu" is the verb for "see".
   "three kings see a farmer" => rusivu vave vulu kupu.
   Subject: three kings (rusivu). Verb: see (vave). Object: a farmer (vulu kupu).
   Here "vave" is the verb for "see" with plural subject.
   "three hills hear two hills" => lasovivu vave lasovivu lu lavula.
   Subject: three hills (lasovivu). Verb: hear (vave). Object: two hills (lasovivu lu).
   Here "vave" is the verb for "hear" with plural subject.
   
   Pattern emerging:
   Plural Subject -> Verb ends in "vave"?
   - three lakes carry -> vave
   - three dogs feed -> vave
   - three kings see -> vave
   - three hills hear -> vave
   - two boats see -> ruvukevu (Plural subject "two boats", verb is "ruvukevu"). Wait, "two" is plural.
   - a hill carry -> porirevu (Singular subject).
   - a king hold -> porirevu (Singular subject).
   
   So:
   Singular Subject -> "porirevu" (for carry/hold?) or specific forms?
   Plural Subject -> "vave" (for carry, feed, see, hear).
   But "two boats see" uses "ruvukevu". "Two" is plural. Why not "vave"?
   Maybe "vave" is specifically for 3+?
   "two boats" (2) -> ruvukevu.
   "three lakes" (3) -> vave.
   "three dogs" (3) -> vave.
   "three kings" (3) -> vave.
   "three hills" (3) -> vave.
   "two hills" (2) -> in "two hills hold three hills", subject is "two hills" (lasovivu lu). Verb is "vave".
   Wait, "two hills hold three hills" => lasovivu lu lasovivu vave vevo.
   Subject: two hills (lasovivu lu). Verb: hold (vave).
   So "two" triggers "vave" for "hold", but "two boats" triggers "ruvukevu" for "see".
   This suggests the verb form depends on the verb itself AND the number?
   Or maybe "vave" is a default plural marker for the verb stem?
   Let's look at the stems:
   see: ruvukevu (2 boats), vave (3 kings, 3 hills).
   feed: same (3 dogs).
   hold: porirevu (1 hill, 1 king), vave (2 hills).
   carry: porirevu (1 hill), vave (3 lakes).
   hear: vave (3 hills).
   
   It seems "vave" is the plural form for many verbs, but "see" with "two" is "ruvukevu".
   Maybe "ruvukevu" is the stem for "see" and "vave" is the plural suffix?
   "ruvukevu" + ? -> "ruvukevu" (no change for 2?).
   "porirevu" + ? -> "vave" (complete change?).
   This is confusing. Let's try to isolate the noun phrases first.
   
   Noun analysis:
   boat: vulovu (2 boats), vulovu (3 boats in "three dogs feed three boats" -> vulovu vave).
   dog: kupu (2 dogs), ruvukevu (3 dogs). Wait.
   "two boats see two dogs" => vulovu lu ruvukevu lu kupu.
   Subject: two boats (vulovu lu). Object: two dogs (lu kupu).
   So "kupu" = dog. "vulovu" = boat.
   "three dogs feed three boats" => ruvukevu vave vulovu vave same.
   Subject: three dogs (ruvukevu). Object: three boats (vulovu vave).
   So "ruvukevu" = three dogs. "vulovu" = boats (plural).
   Wait, "two boats" is "vulovu lu". "three boats" is "vulovu vave".
   So "vulovu" is the plural stem for boat? And "lu" marks 2, "vave" marks 3?
   "two dogs" is "lu kupu". "three dogs" is "ruvukevu".
   So "kupu" is singular dog? "lu kupu" = two dogs. "ruvukevu" = three dogs.
   This implies "ruvukevu" is the word for "three dogs" as a phrase, not just "dog".
   But "two boats" is "vulovu lu". "vulovu" is the plural form of boat?
   Let's check "three lakes".
   "three lakes carry a hill" => misilavu vave lasovi kali.
   "a lake feed a stone" (hypothetical).
   "two boats see two dogs" => vulovu lu ...
   Is "misilavu" = three lakes?
   "a lake" would be "misilavu"? No, "a lake" is singular.
   If "misilavu" is "three lakes", then "a lake" might be "misilavu" without number? No, "a" is indefinite singular.
   Maybe "misilavu" is the word for "lake" (plural form?) and "a lake" is something else?
   Let's look at "three kings".
   "three kings see a farmer" => rusivu vave vulu kupu.
   "a king hold two stones" => rusi porirevu lu vevo.
   So "rusi" = a king (singular). "rusivu" = three kings (plural).
   "rusi" vs "rusivu".
   "a hill" = lasovi. "three hills" = lasovivu.
   "a stone" = vevo? "two stones" = lu kali? Wait.
   "a king hold two stones" => rusi porirevu lu vevo.
   "a hill carry two stones" => lasovi porirevu lu kali.
   Here "lu vevo" vs "lu kali".
   In "a king hold two stones", object is "lu vevo".
   In "a hill carry two stones", object is "lu kali".
   Are "vevo" and "kali" both "stone"? Or different stones?
   The prompt says "Nouns: lake, stone, boat, dog, hill, road, farmer, king". Only one "stone".
   So "vevo" and "kali" must be related.
   Maybe "vevo" is "stone" and "kali" is "stone" with a case marker?
   Or maybe "lu kali" is "two stones" and "lu vevo" is "two stones"?
   Wait, "lu" is the number "two".
   So "lu vevo" = two stones. "lu kali" = two stones.
   This implies "vevo" and "kali" are the same word? Or one is singular, one is plural?
   "a king hold two stones" -> "lu vevo".
   "a hill carry two stones" -> "lu kali".
   Maybe the verb affects the object?
   Or maybe "vevo" is "stone" and "kali" is "stone" in a different case?
   Let's look at "three lakes carry a hill" => ... lasovi kali.
   Here "lasovi" is "a hill". "kali" is attached to it?
   "lasovi kali" = a hill.
   "lasovi" = hill. "kali" = ?
   In "a hill carry two stones" => lasovi porirevu lu kali.
   Here "lu kali" = two stones.
   So "kali" appears in "two stones" and "a hill".
   Maybe "kali" is a suffix for plural?
   "lu kali" = two stones. "lasovi kali" = a hill? That doesn't fit "a hill" being singular.
   Unless "lasovi kali" means "the hill" (definite)? But the English says "a hill".
   Wait, "three lakes carry a hill" => misilavu vave lasovi kali.
   If "lasovi" is "hill", then "lasovi kali" is "a hill".
   But "lu kali" is "two stones".
   Maybe "kali" is the plural marker for "stone"?
   "lu" = two. "kali" = stone (plural)?
   Then "lu kali" = two stones.
   What about "a stone"?
   "a king hold two stones" => rusi porirevu lu vevo.
   Here "lu vevo" = two stones.
   Why "vevo" here and "kali" there?
   Maybe "vevo" is "stone" and "kali" is "stone" + something?
   Or maybe "vevo" is "stone" and "kali" is "hill"?
   Let's re-evaluate "lasovi kali".
   If "lasovi" is hill, and "lasovi kali" is "a hill", then "kali" is not part of the noun phrase for "hill" in the singular?
   Wait, "three lakes carry a hill" => misilavu vave lasovi kali.
   Maybe "lasovi" is "hill" and "kali" is the object marker?
   But in "a hill carry two stones", "lasovi" is subject, "lu kali" is object.
   If "lu kali" is "two stones", then "kali" is "stone".
   Then "lasovi kali" in "three lakes carry a hill" would be "hill stone"? That makes no sense.
   Unless the word order is different?
   "misilavu vave lasovi kali".
   If SVO: Subject (misilavu) Verb (vave) Object (lasovi kali).
   Object = "lasovi kali". If "lasovi" is hill, what is "kali"?
   Maybe "lasovi" is "hill" and "kali" is a suffix indicating "a" (indefinite)?
   But "a hill" in "a hill carry two stones" is just "lasovi".
   Contradiction.
   Let's reconsider the nouns.
   Maybe "lasovi" is not "hill".
   "a hill carry two stones" => lasovi porirevu lu kali.
   "three hills hear two hills" => lasovivu vave lasovivu lu lavula.
   "two hills hold three hills" => lasovivu lu lasovivu vave vevo.
   "lasovivu" clearly relates to "hill". "lasovivu" = three hills. "lasovivu lu" = two hills.
   So "lasovi" is likely the singular "hill".
   Then "lasovi kali" in "three lakes carry a hill" must mean "a hill".
   Why is "kali" there?
   Maybe "kali" is the indefinite article marker for the object?
   But "a hill" as subject is "lasovi" (no "kali").
   "a king" is "rusi". "three kings" is "rusivu".
   "a lake" is unknown. "three lakes" is "misilavu".
   "a stone" is unknown. "two stones" is "lu kali" or "lu vevo".
   "a boat" is unknown. "two boats" is "vulovu lu". "three boats" is "vulovu vave".
   "a dog" is unknown. "two dogs" is "lu kupu". "three dogs" is "ruvukevu".
   "a farmer" is unknown. "a farmer" in "three kings see a farmer" is "vulu kupu".
   "a road" is unknown.
   
   Let's look at "lu kupu" (two dogs) vs "ruvukevu" (three dogs).
   "lu" = two. "kupu" = dog.
   "ruvukevu" = three dogs.
   So "kupu" is singular dog. "ruvukevu" is plural (3) dogs.
   "vulovu lu" (two boats). "vulovu" = boats (plural). "lu" = two.
   "vulovu vave" (three boats). "vave" = three?
   "lasovivu" (three hills). "lasovi" (hill). "lasovivu" = three hills.
   "rusivu" (three kings). "rusi" (king). "rusivu" = three kings.
   "misilavu" (three lakes).
   "lasovivu" (three hills).
   "rusivu" (three kings).
   "ruvukevu" (three dogs).
   It seems the pattern for "three" is adding "vu" or changing the stem?
   "lasovi" -> "lasovivu".
   "rusi" -> "rusivu".
   "kupu" -> "ruvukevu" (big change).
   "vulovu" -> "vulovu vave" (adds "vave").
   "misilavu" (no singular form seen yet).
   
   Let's look at "two".
   "lu kupu" (two dogs).
   "lu kali" (two stones).
   "lu vevo" (two stones).
   "lu lasovivu" (two hills).
   "lu vulovu" (two boats).
   So "lu" is the number "two".
   And it seems to attach to the noun.
   "lu kupu" = two dogs.
   "lu kali" = two stones.
   "lu vevo" = two stones.
   Why two forms for "two stones"?
   Maybe "vevo" and "kali" are different words for stone? No, only one stone.
   Maybe "vevo" is "stone" and "kali" is "stone" in a specific context?
   Or maybe "lu kali" is "two stones" and "lu vevo" is "two stones" but the verb changes the object form?
   In "a hill carry two stones" (verb: porirevu) -> "lu kali".
   In "a king hold two stones" (verb: porirevu) -> "lu vevo".
   Same verb "porirevu", different object forms "lu kali" vs "lu vevo".
   This suggests "porirevu" might not be the same verb, or the object form depends on the subject?
   Subject: "a hill" (lasovi) -> "lu kali".
   Subject: "a king" (rusi) -> "lu vevo".
   Maybe "vevo" is "stone" and "kali" is "stone" + case?
   Or maybe "vevo" is "stone" and "kali" is "hill"?
   Wait, "lasovi kali" in "three lakes carry a hill".
